plainMeaning: May my respect reach, at all times, the twenty living Tīrthaṅkaras—led by Sīmandhara—who are teaching right now in other parts of the universe.
recitation: sīmandhara-pramukha vīśa viharamāna, jinane mārī trikāla vandanā hojo.

wordByWord.gloss: headed by Sīmandhara; with Sīmandhara foremost
wordByWord.etymology: bahuvrīhi compound: Sīmandhara (proper name of the best-known living Jina) + pramukha 'chief, foremost, at the head' (Skt. pra- 'forward' + mukha 'face'), i.e. 'those whose foremost one is Sīmandhara'.
wordByWord.notes: Sīmandhara-svāmī is named because he is the viharamāna-jina most invoked in Śvetāmbara devotion; he stands for the whole set.

wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati cardinal 'twenty', from Skt./Prakrit viṁśati (viṁśa-). Fixes the canonical count of viharamāna Jinas present at one time.

wordByWord.gloss: currently moving about and teaching; presently living
wordByWord.etymology: present middle participle of Skt. vi-√hṛ 'to wander, move about, dwell', via Prakrit viharamāṇa. Distinguishes Jinas now active in their bodies from those already liberated.
jina 'conqueror (of the passions), Tīrthaṅkara' (Skt. √ji 'to conquer') + Gujarati dative postposition -ne 'to'; here a collective/honorific dative for all twenty.
wordByWord.notes: The whole epithet-phrase sīmandhara-pramukha vīśa viharamāna qualifies this dative noun.

wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati first-person possessive (feminine, agreeing with vandanā), from Skt. madīya / mama. Marks the homage as personal and first-person.

wordByWord.gloss: of the three times (past, present, future); threefold
wordByWord.etymology: tri 'three' + kāla 'time'; functions adverbially with vandanā—homage offered for all three times.
Skt. vandanā, from √vand 'to praise, salute, revere'. The act of bowing in devotion.
Gujarati optative/benedictive of hovuṁ 'to be' (from Skt. √bhū). Turns the line into a wish—'let my homage be (offered)'—rather than a statement.
wordByWord.notes: The same hojo benedictive paces the parallel trikāla-vandanā lines of the surrounding verses.

literalTranslation: To the twenty currently-living Jinas, headed by Sīmandhara—may my threefold (past-present-future) homage be.
idiomaticTranslation: To Sīmandhara-svāmī and the twenty Jinas now living and teaching across the worlds, may my homage go out at all three times—past, present, and forever.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: Where the preceding line bows to every monk, nun, and lay devotee who upholds the Vītarāga's command across the two-and-a-half continents, this verse lifts the gaze beyond our own region of the cosmos to the Jinas who are alive at this very moment. The grammar is deliberately spare: a single dative noun, jinane, weighed down by a three-word epithet—sīmandhara-pramukha vīśa viharamāna—and then the bare wish, trikāla vandanā hojo. Naming Sīmandhara first (pramukha) is not arbitrary; in Śvetāmbara devotion he is the living Jina to whom the soul of this avasarpiṇī can still reach out, the assurance that the path is not closed merely because no Tīrthaṅkara is born in Bharata-kṣetra now. The number vīśa (twenty) is doctrinally fixed: that many viharamāna Jinas are held to be present in the Mahāvideha regions at any one time, their omniscience and preaching uninterrupted. The benedictive hojo keeps the line a longing rather than a boast—the reciter does not claim to have bowed, but asks that the bow be carried, by intention, to teachers he will never physically see. Set between homage to the present Saṅgha (before) and homage to the past, future, and present cauvīsīs (after), this verse is the bridge from 'those near me now' to 'every Jina across space', completing the spatial reach of the salutation just as the next verse completes its reach across time.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse rests on the Jain cosmology of simultaneous Tīrthaṅkaras: while Bharata-kṣetra's twenty-four have all attained mokṣa, the Mahāvideha-kṣetras—where the conditions for a Tīrthaṅkara never lapse—always hold living, teaching Jinas, conventionally numbered twenty. Honouring a viharamāna-jina therefore affirms two principles at once: that omniscient guidance is perpetually available somewhere in the loka, and that vandanā is efficacious by sincere intention across any distance the body cannot cross. The trikāla qualifier extends the act beyond a single moment, so that one finite bow is offered as continuous devotion.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: As the sāmāyika closes, the reciter widens the equanimity just practised outward—directing it not only to people nearby but to the highest living teachers anywhere in existence. Practically, it trains the mind to revere virtue regardless of distance, and to hold Sīmandhara-svāmī as a present, reachable refuge that keeps aspiration alive even in an age with no Tīrthaṅkara on this earth.

sources.title: Jain eLibrary (JainQQ) — entries on Sīmandhara-svāmī and the viharamāna Jinas of Mahāvideha
sources.consultedFor: Identity of Sīmandhara-svāmī, the fixed count of twenty currently-living Jinas, and the Mahāvideha-kṣetra context.

sources.title: Cologne Digital Sanskrit Dictionaries (Monier-Williams)
sources.consultedFor: Roots and senses of pramukha (pra-mukha), vi-√hṛ → viharamāṇa, √vand → vandanā, and tri-kāla.

sources.title: Simandhara — encyclopedic overview of the living Tīrthaṅkara and the twenty viharamāna Jinas
sources.consultedFor: Cross-check of Sīmandhara's status as a presently-living Jina and the doctrine of twenty contemporaneous Tīrthaṅkaras in Mahāvideha.

notes: The verse is linguistically plain Gujarati; all eight tokens are glossed (100% coverage). 'vīśa' (twenty) is the doctrinally standard count of viharamāna Jinas and is not in textual doubt. The exact spelling સીમંધર/વીશ is preserved verbatim per the book's orthography rules. Sīmandhara's role as 'pramukha' reflects devotional prominence, not a claim of seniority over the other nineteen. The cited URLs are general repositories/encyclopedic pages rather than a single critical edition; treat the precise list of the twenty names as tradition-level rather than fixed by this book.
